settings.welcome: Hi! I'm TranscriptBot. Ask me anything about the videos.

catalyst.name: Boosting your learning transformation.txt
catalyst.content: Congratulations, you've now got your learning archetype, which means that you're actually now one of the handful of people in the world who know what's really working well for them and what they need to improve on. This took me a few years to figure out. And if your scores or your archetype isn't exactly what you expected, don't worry, we all start the journey from somewhere. And what matters is not your actual level, it's about you getting relatively better than wherever you are now. Going from a level one to a level two doesn't sound very impressive, but doubling your current ability That's what you've got to look forward to. Now, I'm going to spend a bit of time to explain to you how you can actually use those insights to make a clear path towards your goals, whether that is to pass an exam or to get a new job or to get some promotion or to just be more confident and tackle your goals with more certainty and in less time. And there are a few realizations that I had as someone that has gone through this journey that would have made it a lot easier for me. And while it's probably not possible to make becoming an ultra-efficient learner literally easy, there are some valuable realizations that I had that will make it easier for you. The first thing is recognizing that your archetype is a snapshot of your current habits. It is not fixed. It is not like a horoscope. It is just a current picture of the processes that you are used to right now. And it can and will change over time. And so what will happen is that as you learn new skills and as your processes evolve, as old habits are untrained and new habits are trained to replace them, your skills and your processes and your habits and your archetype will start to evolve with it. So you actually will have to reevaluate and redo that assessment every few months. The path of growth and towards becoming a great learner is less of a straight road that you just walk down. And more like rock climbing, where you hold on to a few things and you look to see where you're going to hold on to next. And then you reach out, you pull on it a little bit, you see if that's good. And you're sort of planning the next few moves at a time. The second thing is to realize that there is a easier and faster path for growth and developing skills and then there is a harder more stressful more risky as in you're less likely to succeed path of trying to improve your skills which is actually more common. And a big part of that is because what we're working with are habits. And habits are really powerful because once you train habits, they are like autopilot. So for me now, thinking and learning in a very efficient way is very habitual to me because of the practice that I have. And so it takes me active effort to try to learn less efficiently because that's not how my brain is used to thinking anymore. But on the flip side, habits are also very sticky. They're powerful because once you've got them, you can rely on them and depend on them very easily day in, day out. But that same property means that if you have a habit that you want to remove, it's very hard to move it. And so I viscerally remember a time in my life where thinking in this way and training myself to think in this way was very, very difficult. And so what I want to share with you are a few tips that can help you to speed up this process so that you can untrain old habits and learn new habits without spending years like I did. The first major principle for fast tracking your growth I want to share is that Fast growth equals a little bit of theory combined with a lot of practice. So let's say that I teach you a new method of note taking. I'll teach you this underlying theory behind it, why it works, how to use it, and then there's a series of instructions. So it might take you, let's say 45 minutes to wrap your head around that technique and learn it for the first time. Now that would be considered the theory. So we've spent 45 minutes to learn that theory. Until we practice it enough for it to become part of our procedural knowledge and then develop into a habit, we haven't actually received the benefit of that technique. It's kind of like saying, hey, there's this way that you can make a million dollars. Until you actually do it, you don't have that money. You don't become a millionaire because you know of a way to become a millionaire. And one of the mistakes that I made and I commonly see people make even in our program is underestimating the amount of practice you actually need to turn this new theory into something that is habit. And this is really important because if we progress too quickly and what happens is that we get more and more theory. So let's say we're now learning two techniques, three techniques, four different techniques. And in total, that may have only taken us three hours. So in three hours, we learned four techniques. We now need to practice those four techniques. But here's the part about learning is that when you're learning and learning in a system, each part affects each other. So how you execute on one technique affects how you execute on another one. If something didn't go right, is it because of this part? Or is it because of this part? Or is it because of this other technique? Or is it because of these two techniques combined? You can see that it becomes much, much harder to realize why you made certain mistakes and then fix them when you're working on so many different things at the same time. you really wanna get to a point where you've learned one or maybe two things that preferably don't overlap with each other at all. For example, a method of scheduling, which has to do with your time management, versus a method of note-taking. You can see how those two techniques wouldn't overlap as much as, for example, trying to learn three different types of note-taking techniques at the same time. And so we normally recommend a ratio of at least one to five theory versus practice. So if there's something that took you 45 minutes to learn the theory for, you should spend at least, let's round that up to around four to five hours of practice using that technique that you've just learned. And this is four to five dedicated, committed hours. It's not just generally having it in the back of your mind while you still try to study the normal way. It's like really trying to focus on learning it. And this is because this time is needed for your brain to slowly start learning the right way to do it. And then those neural pathways to start connecting together. It's a very slow, gradual process. And Trying to do this faster is almost 100% guaranteed to make you learn it incorrectly and cause issues later down the line, because now you have a bunch of techniques that are being done incorrectly, all creating different sorts of problems, which you now have to deal with. And you're gonna end up in the unfortunate position of knowing lots of different techniques, knowing you're also doing those techniques not correctly, not getting the benefit from them. And so actually your attempt to get better at learning is going to make you worse. as in it would have been better for you to not even have learned it in the first place because at least then you have just less to think about. In some cases, especially the later techniques that are much more nuanced and specific and advanced and talking about quite specific applications of multiple basic skills coming together in a very certain way, the ratio of theory to practice could be 40 times. It may only take you 20 minutes to wrap your head around how you're meant to do a certain technique. In principle, it's simple, but to actually pull it off with mastery, it's kind of like playing a sport. The amount of time you have to spend drilling and practicing to be able to execute it, not just in training, but in real world pressured situations, is very, very high. And again, for that reason, those types of techniques that require 40 times the amount of practice, those are later in the program. Because again, those are the things that can be life-changing. but take you much longer to learn and therefore it's less likely to give you value in that short term and solve your current problems. Now here's the good thing though, is that let's say that you're spending two hours, two to three hours a week on learning new theory, which is a good amount of time to spend on this program, I think. If you're spending two to three hours a week learning theory, then based on this, you're needing to spend at least around 15 hours a week on practice. And so you might be thinking, well, where am I going to get 15 hours a week to practice when I'm already so busy with studying? And this is another incredibly important point is that you already have those hours. the practice time is mostly, mostly already overlapping with the time you already spend on studying. So let's say that you're currently a university student that's studying, let's say 20 hours a week. you're already spending 20 hours a week studying. And we're gonna teach you techniques that help to replace what you're already doing. In some cases, adding new techniques to do every now and again, but you're already spending 20 hours a week. So it's not about saying, I'm gonna spend 20 hours a week studying and then 15 hours a week practicing. The practice and the study are one and the same because the techniques are designed to help you get better at studying straight away. So now in your 20 hours of studying, 15 of those hours end up being dedicated to practicing. And so while you're practicing, you're also learning your content and studying it. It replaces your current system, kind of like thinking about replacing parts of a car. One part is removed and then it's replaced by another part. We don't have to go and buy a whole new car to be building. So what do you do if you don't have anything to study right now? Let's say that you're on a break or you're trying to get ahead. You can use whatever material that you want in that case, but you still need to practice. In this situation, you do need to spend additional time that you wouldn't normally spend. The practice is necessary. And so you may have a copy of the material you're about to go into for the next year, or you can build your own curriculum using AI, or you can find just some Wikipedia articles to just learn things. You can learn something that you've always wanted to try learning as a hobby. And even if what you choose to learn is not an exact replica of what you're going to need to learn later, even if there's 50% overlap, that's a 50% head start. But the primary objective is that we're developing our skill. And so over time, let's say if we're starting from 20 hours a week of studying, maybe with all the techniques that we're learning and the practice that we're doing and the additional time we need for reflection and just thinking about it, that 20 hours a week may go up to 25 hours a week. A little bit of extra time spent because you're having to think a little bit more, you're having to reflect a little bit more. And then as time goes on and you become more efficient because the techniques are more efficient, you're able to cover the same amount of content with a higher level of knowledge and a higher mastery in a shorter period of time. And so you're actually saving time through efficiency. So that 25 hours a week becomes 20 hours again, and then eventually 15 hours and then 10 hours. So your growth, what ends up happening is that if we make a little graph like this, where we've got the amount of time you're spending against your level of expertise. Initially, the time that you're spending actually goes up a little bit as you're trying to develop that expertise. And then because as you get better and better, it becomes more and more efficient. This starts coming back down until you are vastly more efficient than when you started. So this part here, this is kind of your investment. And this here, this is the benefit and efficiency that you gain. And how long this process overall takes strongly depends on how much of this theory and practice that you're balancing. Too much theory and then you'll move too slow because you're overloaded and you're making too many mistakes. not enough theory and you're not actually learning anything new to practice and you're just doing more of the same thing. Based on our experience with the thousands of students that we've coached, we normally recommend a maximum of five hours of theory per week because beyond five hours of new theory, the amount of practice you need and the complexity becomes so high that it is realistically not going to be achievable for 99% of people. Remember that in those five hours of theory, you will be learning things that may have taken me two or three months to figure out. So while it might only be five hours, there is a lot of density to what you're learning. Now, the next point is to understand how long will it take for you to reach your goal and with this trajectory. So let's just do another quick graph. This time we've got time on the X axis and we've got our level of expertise. Let's say level of skill on the Y axis. If we're currently starting from, let's say, a 2 out of 10, level of skill, but in order to achieve our goal, we need to hit a eight out of 10 level of skill. Then obviously this is going to take longer than someone who is starting from let's say a five. out of 10 level of skill. This person simply needs to improve like this, whereas this other person at the same rate of improvement would take twice as long. And so what this means is that if the deadline for your goal, so let's say you need to hit this eight out of 10, but you only have, let's say, four weeks to hit it. Well, if you were starting at a five out of 10, then that four weeks, it may be fine. You may be able to hit that. However, if you're starting from a 2 out of 10, you may only hit 6 out of 10 by the time that 4-week time is up. This is, in a way, just how it is. I promise you that the way that I'm trying to teach you to learn to be a better learner is the fastest way that I know of. the fastest and safest way in that it's the highest chance of success in the shortest amount of time with the greatest amount of certainty based on my years of experience coaching. And I'm sure 10 years from now, I'll have an even faster and better way, which I haven't discovered yet. And it may mean that the time it takes for you to become a great learner is longer than how long you've got left. And this is obvious. Like if you're trying to become an amazing learner and you haven't studied anything for five months and you've got an exam tomorrow, and right now you feel like you're completely behind on everything, That's not a realistic expectation to have. That's an extreme example, but that's obvious. All I'm saying is that it's a spectrum. And so maybe even one week is not enough. Maybe two weeks is not enough. Maybe three weeks is not enough. It really depends on where your current level is. And if you feel like you're not sure if it's on the edge, do your best. Give yourself the best chance. You're going to have to go through this journey anyway. If you want to have a life where you're doing the things that you want, engaging in the opportunities that you want, able to spend time with your friends and your loved ones and have a prosperous career. These are great goals to have. And most people will never be able to achieve that because most people can't be bothered starting on that journey. But for you who has joined this program, I want you to be bothered. I want you to go on this journey and I want to be able to help you with that because it's your life we're talking about and it's worth it to do hard things in life if it helps us live the life we want to. I felt overwhelmed many times going through this journey and I doubted myself many times and I didn't have anyone to guide me through this process. So I want to be able to guide you through it, make it a little bit easier for you. And one of the things that would have made it much easier, at least mentally, for me to continue going through this journey and to just make faster progress with more certainty is understanding that progress is more important than potential. And then when I started working as a doctor, I had to upgrade it again. And then, you know, eventually I upgraded it enough times until a point where, for example, when I did my master of education, I was able to fly through that and get the top marks in the entire year. Very... easily in a way. It was not as challenging as I thought it would have been. And that's because I'd spent years and years thoroughly refining my learning system. But the point here is that for me, that journey took 10 years. And it isn't necessary for you to get to that end point from day one. I didn't need this, the perfect learning system to enter into medical school. I just needed one that was good enough. I didn't need the perfect one to go through medical school while working full-time. I needed one that was good enough. I didn't need the perfect learning system to start a business and do the coaching and work two full-time jobs while also working as a junior doctor. I needed one that was good enough. At that point, it needed to be pretty good. And so this is what I mean by progress is more important than potential. Most of the time for our goals, we don't need to have the maximum, you know, unleash the maximum genetic potential, you know, in our brains. We don't need to be a Nobel Prize winning physicist to pass our... first year university physics exam. And if right now you're facing problems like issues with your memory or forgetting things or inconsistency in exams or performance, lack of confidence about studying new material, getting overwhelmed, procrastinating, issues with time management, procrastination, whatever the issues are, These problems that you're facing right now, you don't need the perfect solution to solve all of them at once. What makes your life easier is having less problems. So even if you go from 10 problems to just having nine problems and then eight problems, your life is getting easier every step of the way. Things are getting better for you. Your performance is improving every step of the way. If you're 1% better today than you were tomorrow, it means you have 1% less problems to deal with today.
